pattern: Once the piles are ordered smallest to largest, the best triplets pair a big pile with a small one to sacrifice to your friend, which leaves a very simple predictable pattern for exactly which piles land in your own hand.
hints: Sort the piles first. Handing your friend the very smallest remaining pile every single time is never a bad trade, because it is the least valuable pile you could possibly give away.

If you always let your friend take the smallest pile still available and always let the other picker take the largest pile still available, the pile that ends up in your hand each round is the second largest of what remains.

After sorting, this pairing pattern means the piles you keep are always exactly those at one-third of the way up the sorted list through two-thirds of the way up, taken every other position -- sum that fixed slice directly instead of simulating round by round.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible input, three equal piles
three piles spanning the full value range
shuffled order that still sorts to a clean run of nine
two clusters of equal values, low and high

stress.description: random piles at a large multiple-of-three size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n3 = max(1, size // 3)
    length = n3 * 3
    piles = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(length)]
    return [piles]
stress.note: size is rounded down to a multiple of three, matching the piles.length % 3 == 0 guarantee

complexity.time: O(n log n) for the sort
